title: Corporate Change Agents: Kelly Ortberg at Boeing and Lip-Bu Tan at Intel
content:
When Kelly Ortberg came out of retirement to take the helm at Boeing, the iconic aerospace manufacturer was facing an unprecedented crisis. The company had suffered a catastrophic loss of public trust following multiple devastating aircraft crashes and highly publicized manufacturing defects. Previous leadership had prioritized aggressive share buybacks and short-term financial gains over essential investments in engineering and long-term innovation. Recognizing the desperate need for a massive cultural overhaul, the board of directors appointed Ortberg as a definitive corporate change agent. Despite his remarkably understated demeanor and low public profile, he quickly began orchestrating one of the most dramatic corporate comebacks on record.

As a trained engineer himself, Ortberg immediately set out to reinstate a strict, engineering-first culture throughout the massive organization. He implemented a methodical, step-by-step approach that specifically targeted major improvements in quality control, mechanical reliability, and on-time delivery. Colleagues noted that his deep technical knowledge allowed him to effectively evaluate operations across every level of the company. Furthermore, he radically changed the corporate incentive structure by directly tying executive pay and promotions to interpersonal respect and collaboration. This new evaluation system successfully dismantled the toxic, overly competitive environment that had plagued the company for years.

Ortberg’s relentless manufacturing revamp is already yielding highly promising financial and operational results across multiple divisions. Under his steady guidance, the company recently won a highly coveted military contract for a next-generation fighter program in a stunning upset. Furthermore, his dedicated efforts successfully persuaded federal aviation regulators to lift strict precautionary caps on commercial aircraft production. Although the company still faces lingering supply-chain shortages and complex certification hurdles, employee morale has improved dramatically. If Ortberg continues this successful trajectory, his successor will inherit a significantly healthier and much more innovative aerospace titan.
